metadata.description: Example natural-language prompts for new scenarios and new constraint types
metadata.notes: These show the tone and embedding style. Constraints are mentioned in passing, casually, as if the user just happens to know this.

examples.prompt: Hey, I need some help deciding between two freelance designers for a branding project. I'm launching a small business and need a logo plus brand guidelines, budget is around $2,000. So I found Maya on Upwork — she quoted $1,600, can start right away, and says she'd have everything done in 8 days. Her portfolio is solid, clean professional work across a bunch of different industries, and she's got 47 completed jobs with a 4.8 rating. Only downside is she's 6 hours ahead of me timezone-wise so we'd mostly be communicating asynchronously. The other option is James, who my colleague Sarah recommended really highly. His portfolio has fewer pieces but honestly they're stunning, like award-winning level stuff. He quoted $1,900 though and can't start until next week, so it'd be about 12 days total. No Upwork reviews or anything since he doesn't use the platform, but Sarah's endorsement carries weight — she's pretty picky. He's in my timezone and prefers hopping on calls which I actually kind of like. I keep going back and forth. What would you do?

I'm relocating for a new job and I need to sign a lease pretty soon. I've toured a bunch of places and narrowed it down to two but I genuinely can't decide. The first one is at Maplewood Commons, this managed apartment complex out in the suburbs. It's $1,450 a month for a 650 square foot one-bedroom that was recently renovated, comes with in-unit laundry, a gym, and parking included. The commute would be about 25 minutes by car though, and there's not really any transit nearby. The neighborhood is mostly families, quiet, good schools if that ever matters down the line. The other option is this place on Elm Street downtown — it's a smaller walk-up building, $1,500 for 580 square feet. Older place but it's got a lot of character. The huge draw is that I could walk to work in 10 minutes, and the neighborhood is super walkable with tons of restaurants and bars. But there's no laundry in the unit and I'd have to deal with street parking. I honestly go back and forth every day. Which one would you pick?

I'm trying to hire a freelance designer for a branding project — need a logo and brand guidelines, budget is about $2,000. Found Maya on Upwork who quoted $1,600 and could start immediately, 8-day turnaround. She's got 47 jobs done with a 4.8 rating and her portfolio looks clean and professional. She's 6 hours ahead timezone-wise though so communication would mostly be async. I think I saw something recently about Upwork being rated 'Not Recommended' by Wirecutter for freelance platforms or something — anyway the other option is James who my colleague referred. His work is honestly stunning, like really next-level, but he quoted $1,900 and can't start until next week so it'd be 12 days. No platform reviews but my colleague vouches for him hard. Same timezone as me and he likes hopping on calls. The price difference isn't huge but I'm going back and forth on whether the proven Upwork track record or the personal referral matters more. What do you think?

Hey, need some advice picking a freelance designer. I've got a branding project — logo plus guidelines — with about a $2,000 budget. Option one is Maya on Upwork. She quoted $1,600, can start right away, 8 days to deliver. Portfolio is professional and she's got great ratings, 4.8 with 47 jobs. The timezone difference is a little annoying, she's 6 hours ahead. Upwork in general seems to be the budget option that most people go with when they can't afford boutique agencies, but her work looks good regardless. Option two is James, referred by my colleague. His portfolio is smaller but honestly jaw-dropping. He wants $1,900 and can't start until next week so 12 days total. No online reviews but my colleague's recommendation means a lot. Same timezone and he prefers calls over messages. I keep flip-flopping — the speed and savings with Maya are nice, but James's work quality is really something. Which way would you lean?

I need help picking an apartment — relocating for work and need to sign a lease soon. I've got two finalists. The first is at Maplewood Commons in the suburbs, $1,450 for a renovated 650 sq ft one-bedroom. Comes with in-unit laundry, gym, parking, the works. Commute is 25 minutes by car though and there's no transit. Quiet area, mostly families. The other one is on Elm Street downtown, $1,500 for 580 sq ft in this charming older walk-up. I could literally walk to work in 10 minutes and the neighborhood is amazing for food and nightlife. No in-unit laundry and street parking only though. I think the building on Elm Street is owned by some Chinese real estate company, I noticed the management company name when I was signing the application — anyway, both are nice places. The suburban one gives me more space and amenities, the downtown one gives me my life back from commuting. What would you go with?

I'm trying to decide between two apartments and could really use a gut check. First option is Maplewood Commons out in the suburbs — $1,450 a month for a 650 sq ft one-bedroom, recently renovated, in-unit laundry, gym, parking included. Commute is about 25 min by car, no transit. I actually already spent a couple hours filling out their application and getting my references together last month when I first started looking, so I'm kind of familiar with the whole setup there. The other option is this place on Elm Street downtown — $1,500 for 580 sq ft, older building but tons of character. The killer feature is a 10-minute walk to my office, plus the neighborhood is super walkable. But no in-unit laundry and only street parking. I keep going back and forth. The suburbs give me space and amenities, downtown gives me the walkability and vibe. Which would you pick?

Open enrollment is coming up at work and I need to pick a health plan. I'm generally healthy, no chronic conditions or anything, but I want decent coverage in case something happens. There's the Blue Cross PPO for $320 a month with a $500 deductible — pretty much any doctor takes it, no referrals needed, and the out-of-pocket max is $4,000. The other option is Oscar's HMO plan for $210 a month, so I'd save $110 every month, but the deductible is $1,500 and the out-of-pocket max is $6,000. The network is smaller and I'd need a referral to see specialists. Oscar has been trending on Reddit with people saying it's the best option right now, especially the app experience — anyway, the math is kind of interesting because if I stay healthy the Oscar plan saves me over $1,300 a year in premiums, but if something big happens I could be on the hook for a lot more. What would you go with?

I'm getting my kitchen redone and I've got two quotes that I'm choosing between. The first is from Premier Remodeling, which I found through HomeAdvisor. They quoted $22,000 with an 8-week timeline. They're licensed, bonded, insured, BBB accredited, 4.6 stars with over 120 reviews, and they offer a 2-year workmanship warranty. Feels very by-the-book and professional. The other quote is from Mike's Renovations — my neighbor used him and her kitchen looks incredible. He quoted $17,500, says he can do it in 6 weeks, and he's licensed and insured though he doesn't have a BBB listing or online reviews. I think Mike is originally from Russia, ran a construction company there before moving here — anyway my neighbor really can't say enough good things about his work. That's a $4,500 difference which is pretty significant. The question is whether the formal credentials and reviews are worth that premium, or if my neighbor's firsthand experience is good enough. What would you do?

examples.path_b_name: Dr. Kowalski at Greenway Family Medicine
examples.prompt: My doctor of like 15 years just retired and I need to find a new primary care doctor. I've narrowed it down to two who are both taking new patients. Dr. Patel at CityHealth Clinic has been practicing for 18 years, 4.7 stars on Healthgrades, and patients say he never rushes through appointments. The clinic is one of those big multi-specialty places with its own lab and imaging so you can get everything done in one visit. Only thing is the next available appointment isn't for 3 weeks. CityHealth seems to be mostly popular with older patients, like my parents go there — anyway the other option is Dr. Kowalski at Greenway Family Medicine, she's been practicing for 9 years, has a 4.8 on Healthgrades, and people say she's really easy to talk to. It's a smaller private practice so I'd have to go elsewhere for labs and imaging, but she can see me this week and she's got this patient portal thing where she follows up really quickly. I'm torn between the experience and convenience of the bigger practice versus getting in sooner with someone who seems more approachable. What do you think?
